critic_raw:
This experiment compares a bidirectional LSTM versus logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction using recall at 80% precision as the primary metric.

The recall at 80% precision primary metric requires setting a decision threshold on the validation set such that precision equals 80%, then freezing this threshold for test evaluation. The problem is that the validation set precision at a given threshold is a random variable. With a validation set of ~30,000 × 0.17 ≈ 5,000 records and approximately 15–20% positive rate, the precision at any fixed threshold has a standard error of approximately sqrt(p(1-p)/n) ≈ 0.006, meaning the 80% precision threshold is estimated to within about ±1%. When this threshold is applied to the test set, the actual test precision may differ from 80% by several percentage points, making the reported 'recall at 80% precision' a recall at an unknown precision that happens to be near 80%. This is a subtle but real evaluation problem.

The bidirectional LSTM uses backward passes through the visit sequence. For readmission prediction, the sequence represents visits in the 12 months prior to the index discharge. A bidirectional LSTM at any internal time step in this sequence will see both earlier and later visits in the backward direction. The later visits (closer to the index discharge) may contain information that is correlated with readmission risk (e.g., escalating lab abnormalities, increasing visit frequency) which is appropriate for unidirectional forward modeling, but backward modeling causes internal representations at early time steps to be informed by the most recent pre-discharge information. This does not constitute label leakage since all visits are pre-discharge, but it does mean the model at earlier time steps is not operating in a causally valid way — which is irrelevant for offline prediction but should be acknowledged.

The logistic regression uses 5-fold cross-validation 'nested within the training set on validation recall' for regularization strength selection. The metric used for nested CV selection is 'validation recall,' which is a threshold-dependent metric requiring a decision threshold. If the threshold for computing recall in nested CV is not specified (e.g., 0.5 by default), the selected regularization strength optimizes recall at an arbitrary threshold, not at the 80% precision operating point. This makes the logistic regression tuning inconsistent with the evaluation objective.

The dataset is described as 200,000 visits, but the unit of analysis is admissions (index visits). The RNN sequence is built from prior visits within 12 months of the index admission. If a patient has multiple index admissions (multiple qualifying hospitalizations within the 36-month period), they contribute multiple training examples. The assignment of 'all records for a single patient to one split' is not described; if patients with multiple index admissions appear in both training and test, visit sequences from the same patient appear in both splits, violating the temporal split design.
all_issues_raised: The 80% precision threshold set on the validation set has estimation variance of approximately ±1% precision at the 5,000-record scale, meaning the reported 'recall at 80% precision' on the test set reflects recall at an unknown precision near 80%, not exactly 80%.

The logistic regression nested CV optimizes recall at an unspecified default threshold (likely 0.5), not at the 80% precision operating point. The tuning objective is inconsistent with the evaluation objective, potentially selecting suboptimal regularization for the actual clinical metric.

Patients with multiple qualifying index admissions in the 36-month period contribute multiple training/test examples. If patient-level assignment to splits is not enforced, visit sequences from the same patient appear in both training and test sets, violating temporal isolation.

Bidirectional LSTM's backward pass at early sequence positions uses information from later pre-discharge visits that are temporally closer to the readmission risk period. While not label leakage, this means internal representations do not correspond to what any causally valid model would compute at those time points.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 80% precision threshold set on the validation set has estimation variance of approximately ±1% precision at the 5,000-record scale, meaning the reported 'recall at 80% precision' on the test set reflects recall at an unknown precision near 80%, not exactly 80%.

The logistic regression nested CV optimizes recall at an unspecified default threshold (likely 0.5), not at the 80% precision operating point. The tuning objective is inconsistent with the evaluation objective, potentially selecting suboptimal regularization for the actual clinical metric.

Patients with multiple qualifying index admissions in the 36-month period contribute multiple training/test examples. If patient-level assignment to splits is not enforced, visit sequences from the same patient appear in both training and test sets, violating temporal isolation.

Bidirectional LSTM's backward pass at early sequence positions uses information from later pre-discharge visits that are temporally closer to the readmission risk period. While not label leakage, this means internal representations do not correspond to what any causally valid model would compute at those time points.
